title: News from Science Weekly Headlines
html_body:
Brought to you by NOMIS & Science Young Explorer Award
This week’s headlines
Help News from Science deliver the deep, trustworthy reporting that this challenging time requires.
Support nonprofit science journalism—make a tax-deductible gift.
Latest News
These small African antelopes may help mpox spread
Researchers find evidence that duikers, hunted and eaten across sub-Saharan Africa, can harbor the deadly virus
By KAI KUPFERSCHMIDT  |  27 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Two years after it emerged, ‘cow flu’ is still circulating—and baffling scientists
Researchers still aren’t sure how H5N1 influenza spreads between cows and from farm to farm
By JON COHEN  |  27 MAR
Read more
Latest News
These birds suck—literally
Scientists spot first example of suction feeding in the avian world
By ERIK STOKSTAD  |  27 MAR
Read more
Latest News
‘Milestone’ research method measures gene activity across whole mice
New way to analyze frozen tissue slices could reveal bodywide effects of drugs, diseases
By CATHERINE OFFORD  |  27 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Jupiter’s weather forecast: cloudy with a chance of nukes
The planet’s lightning storms can unleash the force of multiple nuclear weapons every minute
By ROBIN GEORGE ANDREWS  |  27 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Abnormal behaviors in lab monkeys may reflect a lifetime of stressful experiences
Pacing and hair pulling may develop after multiple experiments, years of relative isolation, and more
By JACK TAMISIEA  |  26 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Unprecedented footage shows sperm whales joining forces to help a newborn calf
Cetaceans from different families take turns lifting baby whale to surface to breathe
By PERRI THALER  |  26 MAR
Read more
News Feature
‘Resurrection plants’ bounce back after years of drought. Do they hold lessons for crops?
Plant biologist Jill Farrant hopes “desiccation-tolerant” species can teach her how to make crops more resilient
26 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Long-standing volcanic eruption theory might be backward
Eruptions could be triggered when bubbles vanish into magma—not when they burst out
By HANNAH RICHTER  |  26 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Hot things can freeze faster than cool ones. Now, this paradox has gone quantum
Unified theory explains Mpemba effect from ice cream to atoms—with implications for faster quantum computing
By ZACK SAVITSKY  |  25 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
NASA revives next-generation flagship Earth-observing missions
“Falcon” and “Eagle” will monitor changing clouds and map critical minerals
By PAUL VOOSEN  |  25 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Trump’s new science panel is stuffed with high-tech billionaires
The PCAST roster contains few academics and women
By JEFFREY MERVIS  |  25 MAR
Read more
Latest News
World’s oldest dog identified at ancient hunter-gatherer site
Bones of 15,800-year-old puppy push confirmed origin of our canine companions back nearly 5000 years
By DAVID GRIMM  |  25 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Genes from giant viruses help polar algae survive frigid waters and harsh sunlight
A prior infection may create lasting, beneficial evolutionary change in these hardy microbes
By AMANDA HEIDT  |  25 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
NASA plans to send a nuclear-powered spacecraft to Mars in 2028
Spacecraft would release helicopters to explore martian surface
By PAUL VOOSEN  |  24 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Dueling AI agents could reveal keys to restoring consciousness
A new study probes consciousness with models that simulate and classify brain activity. A neuroscientist behind the work explains its ambitions
By JENNIE ERIN SMITH  |  24 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Neanderthals survived on a knife’s edge for 350,000 years
A pair of studies illuminates these humans’ long, hardscrabble existence
By ANDREW CURRY  |  23 MAR
Read more
Latest News
AI algorithms can become ‘agents of chaos’
Given autonomous control of other software, programs shared private medical details and deleted files without permission
By JEFFREY BRAINARD  |  23 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Iran’s internet shutdown muddies global efforts to track damage to historical sites
At least 56 cultural sites have been damaged since strikes began in February—but internet blackouts and academic isolation are obscuring the true toll
By KRISTIN ROMEY  |  23 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
Energy Department merges nuclear and particle physics programs, unsettling researchers
In spite of synergies, physicists worry both fields are being sidelined in favor of AI
By ADRIAN CHO  |  20 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Hormone linked to morning sickness may help reduce alcohol intake
GDF15, which is thought to contribute to nausea during pregnancy, may keep our drinking in check, researchers propose
By CATHERINE OFFORD  |  20 MAR
Read more
Latest News
Microscopic spikes on snakeskin block bacterial buildup
Structures on python scales could inspire chemical-free antimicrobial designs
By ADA MCVEAN  |  20 MAR
Read more
ScienceInsider
First science from private Moon lander challenges lunar divide
Blue Ghost data suggest NASA’s growing commercial Moon program can deliver results
By PAUL VOOSEN  |  20 MAR
Read more
